calendar.matters:
MOTION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE / Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Mandate To Set Aside The Dmv Order Of Revocation
Off Calendar Apr-27-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Sarah Potter of Law Office of Sarah Potter telephone number 415-864-5600 for Petitioner Yi Feng Zhang.
Austin Cattermole of Office of the Attorney General telephone number 510-622-2190 for Respondent Department of Motor Vehicles.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court adopted its tentative ruling. PETITIONER YI ZHANG'S PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE To Set Aside The Dmv Order Of Revocation is denied. This matter is before the court for review of a final administrative decision made as the result of a proceeding in which by law a hearing is required to be given, evidence is required to be taken, and discretion in the determination of facts is vested in the respondent board. (See Veh. Code, 14100 et seq.) Thus, the matter is reviewed by way of petition for administrative and decided by the court sitting without a jury. (See Code Civ. Proc., 1094.5, subd. (a); Veh. Code, 13559.) The inquiry extends to the questions whether the respondent has proceeded without, or in excess of, jurisdiction; whether there was a fair trial; and whether there was any prejudicial abuse of discretion. (See Code Civ. Proc., 1094.5, subd. (b).) Where petitioner claims that the findings are not supported by the evidence, "in cases in which the court is authorized by law to exercise its independent judgment on the evidence, abuse of discretion is established if the court determines that the findings are not supported by the weight of the evidence'." (Code Civ. Proc., 1094.5, subd. (c).) Here, the parties agree that the "independent judgment" standard governs. (See Dep't's MPA at p. 4.) "In exercising its independent judgment, a trial court must afford a strong presumption of correctness concerning the administrative findings, and the party challenging the administrative decision bears the burden of convincing the court that the administrative findings are contrary to the weight of the evidence." (Fukuda v. City of Angels (1999) 20 Cal.4th 805, 817.) Here, the weight of the evidence supports the ALJ's findings that (1) Zhang refused to complete a chemical test and (2) Zhang was advised that a refusal would result in a suspension or revocation of his driving privilege. Officer Bushnell so testified. (See Hrg. TX at pp. 8-10, 12-13, 17 and 18-19.) No witness testified otherwise. The incomplete DS 367 and the absence of a clear reference to a chemical-test advisement in Officer Bushnell's report could support a finding otherwise (see AR Exs. 1 and 2); but, given Officer Bushnell's uncontradicted testimony, the weight of the evidence supports the ALJ's finding that the advisement was given. Order signed in open Court.

Judge: Joseph Quinn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)
